physiologicalParams.name: Bone mineral density
physiologicalParams.typical: Peak bone-building years — up to ~40% of adult bone mass forms in adolescence
physiologicalParams.driver: Calcium and vitamin D intake plus weight-bearing sports

physiologicalParams.name: Height growth velocity
physiologicalParams.typical: Tracking along her growth curve after the early-teen spurt
physiologicalParams.driver: Energy intake matched to growth and daily activity

physiologicalParams.name: Aerobic fitness (estimated VO2max)
physiologicalParams.driver: Soccer, swimming, dance and cycling most days

physiologicalParams.name: Muscular strength & coordination
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: More reps and longer play without fatigue in 6–8 weeks
physiologicalParams.driver: Varied sport practice plus protein spread across meals

physiologicalParams.name: Healthy weight trajectory (BMI-for-age)
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: Tracks smoothly along the growth curve — no restrictive dieting
physiologicalParams.driver: Regular balanced meals sized to activity

physiologicalParams.name: Energy & iron status
physiologicalParams.typical: Teen girls need more iron (about 15 mg/day)
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: Steady energy in class and sport
physiologicalParams.driver: Iron-rich beans, beef, turkey and fortified grains paired with vitamin C fruit

physiologicalParams.name: Sleep duration & quality
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: Easier sleep onset and a more consistent schedule
physiologicalParams.driver: Daytime activity and regular meal timing

physiologicalParams.name: Focus & mood
physiologicalParams.typical: Stable energy across the school day
physiologicalParams.driver: Breakfast every day and steady carbs plus protein at lunch

meals.items: Overnight oats with sliced banana, peanut butter and chia; small glass of orange juice
Tuna salad sandwich on whole-grain bread with lettuce; apple; baby carrots
Greek yogurt parfait with granola and mixed berries
Sheet-pan honey-lime chicken with roasted sweet potatoes and broccoli; whole-grain roll

exercise.activity: Soccer practice with the school team (drills + scrimmage)

week.highlight: A colorful sheet-pan dinner refuels muscles after a hard soccer practice.

meals.items: Greek yogurt with honey, walnuts and sliced peach; slice of whole-grain toast
Chicken pita pocket with tzatziki, cucumber and tomato; small bunch of grapes
Hummus with pita triangles and bell pepper strips; banana
Baked lemon-herb white fish, roasted potatoes with olive oil and oregano, green beans; whole-grain roll

exercise.activity: Basketball with friends at the park

week.highlight: Mediterranean flavors pack in olive oil, fish and plenty of vegetables.

meals.items: Scrambled eggs with whole-grain toast, banana and orange slices; glass of low-fat milk
Chicken and vegetable stir-fry with brown rice (bento-style school lunch)
Steamed edamame, rice crackers and a mandarin
Vegetable and tofu pad thai with rice noodles, crushed peanuts and lime

week.highlight: A plant-forward pad thai shows protein doesn't always have to come from meat.

meals.items: Whole-grain toast with mashed avocado and a fried egg; mango chunks; glass of low-fat milk
Chicken and black bean burrito bowl with brown rice, salsa, lettuce and a sprinkle of cheese
Small corn-tortilla cheese quesadilla; watermelon cubes
Beef fajitas with sauteed peppers and onions, flour tortillas and guacamole

week.highlight: Iron-rich beef and beans help meet the higher iron needs of teen girls.

meals.items: Banana-berry smoothie blended with low-fat milk and oats; small handful of almonds
Chana masala (chickpea curry) with basmati rice and cucumber raita
Apple slices with peanut butter
Chicken tikka with whole-wheat naan, yellow dal and kachumber salad

exercise.activity: Easy jog with the running club + 10 minutes of stretching

week.highlight: Indian-style dishes deliver fiber-rich legumes alongside whole grains.

meals.items: Whole-grain waffles topped with strawberries, yogurt and a drizzle of maple syrup
Caprese sandwich with mozzarella, tomato and basil on ciabatta; fresh peach
Air-popped popcorn with a sprinkle of parmesan; grapes
Homemade whole-wheat veggie pizza with mozzarella, mushrooms and spinach; side salad

exercise.activity: Family bike ride on the greenway

week.highlight: Homemade pizza night keeps food fun while the veggies keep it balanced.

meals.items: Shakshuka-style eggs in tomato-pepper sauce with whole-grain pita; orange
Falafel wrap with hummus, tomato and cucumber; watermelon slices
Dates stuffed with almond butter; glass of low-fat milk
Grilled chicken and vegetable skewers with bulgur pilaf and yogurt-mint sauce

exercise.activity: Gentle yoga at home + relaxed neighborhood walk

week.highlight: A lighter recovery day pairs easy movement with a colorful Middle Eastern spread.

micronutrients.name: Iron
micronutrients.keySources: Beef fajitas, chickpeas, black beans, fortified grains

micronutrients.name: Vitamin D
micronutrients.keySources: Fortified milk and yogurt, eggs, baked fish

nutrition: Calcium- and vitamin D-rich dairy at most meals supports the roughly 40% of adult bone mass built during the teen years.
Roughly 90–110 g of protein a day, spread across four meals, fuels growth spurts and muscle repair after sport.
Iron from beef, beans and fortified grains — paired with vitamin C fruits — helps offset the higher iron needs of teen girls.
Over 30 g of fiber daily from oats, whole grains, fruit and legumes keeps digestion comfortable and fullness steady.
Eating breakfast and regular meals keeps blood sugar even, supporting concentration through the school day.
exercise: Weight-bearing sports like soccer, basketball and dance stimulate bone density during these peak building years.
Five to six varied active days steadily build aerobic fitness — VO2max typically rises 5–10% within 8–12 weeks.
Mixing team sports, swimming, dance and cycling develops coordination, agility and confidence.
One light recovery day of yoga and easy walking builds the habit of rest and lowers injury risk.
Consistent daytime activity makes it easier to fall asleep — important when teens need 8–10 hours a night.

notes: Teenage girls are in peak bone-building years, so calcium, vitamin D and iron matter most. This week matches food to growth and sport — it is about fueling well, never about restrictive dieting.
